summary: Gruber reviews the iPhone 5C and 5S, arguing the 5C is a brilliant marketing play that repackages year-old technology in colorful plastic while still commanding premium prices. He dismantles the 'Apple can't innovate' narrative by showing the same criticisms could have been leveled at products released under Steve Jobs. The 5S review focuses on three pillars: the A7 chip's move to 64-bit ARMv8 architecture delivering 2x performance gains, Touch ID's near-instantaneous fingerprint recognition, and burst mode photography. He makes a detailed technical case that the 64-bit transition is not a marketing gimmick but a genuine architectural improvement. The overall thesis is that real innovation is incremental refinement, not revolution.
key_points: The iPhone 5C is essentially a rebranded iPhone 5 distinguished only by colorful plastic casing, but this is a deliberate and likely successful marketing strategy — in marketing, what looks new is new
The 'Apple can't innovate without Jobs' narrative is demolished by showing that every post-2007 iPhone release, including the iPad, could have been dismissed the same way had Jobs left earlier

The A7's performance gains come primarily from the clean-break ARMv8 instruction set architecture, not from 64-bit addressing alone — critics calling it a gimmick fundamentally misunderstand the technical picture
Touch ID represents Apple's core strength of on-device hardware excellence, contrasting with its weakness in online services, with fingerprint data stored only in secure hardware and never in iCloud
Burst mode photography with intelligent software for selecting and discarding shots is the kind of incremental innovation that gets overlooked but genuinely advances the user experience
money_quotes: In marketing, what looks new is new.
To paraphrase Stanley Kubrick, the truth of the iPhone 5C is in the feel of it, not the think of it.

With the iPhone 5C Apple may well have created what will prove to be the most popular smartphone in the world, based almost entirely on year-old technology, distinguished only by its colorful plastic casing — yet still sold at premium prices compared to the rest of the industry.
The square-pegged facts have been hammered to fit the round-hole narrative.
The iPhone 5S is, in some measures, computationally superior to the top-of-the-line MacBook Pro from just five years ago. In your fucking pocket.
Touch ID is way faster than 'fast enough'. I'd call it 'I can't believe it works this quickly' fast.
Innovation is missed by most people because it is so often incremental.
Refinement, in the eyes of these naysayers, does not count as innovation. Only revolution counts. But the iPhone needs no revolution.
key_insight: Real innovation is most often incremental refinement — the 'Apple can't innovate' narrative mistakes revolution for the only form of progress while ignoring the compounding gains of disciplined iteration.
